concepts.title: Increasing and decreasing functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Monotonicity

There are 2 types of functions according to their behavior / monotonicity.

(1) Increasing function

(2) Decreasing function

We further divide them into 2 subcategories.

(a) strictly increasing function

(b) increasing or non-decreasing function / decreasing or non-increasing function

Increasing functions 

As the name suggests these functions are such that there value at a point on the curve keeps increasing or stays constant as we increase the value of $\text{x}$

. i.e. $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ if $x\ge {{x}_{2}}$

$f\left( {{x}_{1}} \right)\ge f\left( {{x}_{2}} \right)$

Some examples : 

Now let us talk about subcategories.

 

(a) Strictly Increasing function : Since we have used the word "strictly" here, the function is always increasing i.e. its value increases as $\text{x}$ increases.

i.e. as ${{x}_{1}}>{{x}_{2}}$

$f\left( {{x}_{1}} \right)>f\left( {{x}_{2}} \right)$

Note, there is no equality sign in the second inequality.

Necessary condition: 

If we see by the adjacent diagram, the tangents drawn to an increasing function always form acute angles.

Hence the required condition is ${f}'\left( x \right)>0$ for all $x\in \left( a,b \right)$, for a function to be strictly increasing in interval $\left( a,b \right)$.

 

(b) Increasing or non-decreasing : Here the functions value may be constant for some values and then increase and this cycle continues. It is not necessary that value increases for every input.

For example: 

the curve in the above figure is an example of this. The value of curve is constant for the period $\left( \text{a},\text{b} \right)\And \left( \text{c},\text{d} \right)$

Note: The value never decreases though it may remain constant.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} \therefore \text{ As }{{\text{x}}_{1}}>{{\text{x}}_{2}} \\ \text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\ge \text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

(2) Decreasing functions : As the same suggests the value of function keeps on decreasing as the value of $x$ increase.

(a) Strictly decreasing function : In this case the value of the function decreases continuously as the value of $\text{x}$ increase.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore \text{ As }{{\text{x}}_{1}} >{{\text{x}}_{2}} \\ \text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right) <\text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

note the change in equality sign.

Example: 
 

Also note the tangent to such curves always forms obtuse angles that means $\frac{dy}{dx}$ or ${f}'\left( x \right)=$ $\text{tan}\theta <0$ as $\theta $ is obtuse.

Necessary Condition : Hence the necessary condition required is $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)<0$ for all $\text{x}$.

(b) Decreasing or non-decreasing functions : Here the value of function might be constant for some period & then will reduce and this cycle goes on but remember never the value of function starts increasing.

 

Example: 

Notice that during the interval (a, b) & (c, d) the value remains constant whereas it has decreasing values for other inputs.

So the required condition is $\text{ as } {{\text{x}}_{1}}>{{\text{x}}_{2}}$
$\text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\le \text{f}\left( {{\text{x}}_{2}} \right)$

Note the equality sign.

In case we find out that ${f}'\left( x \right)$ is greater than zero in some part of domain & less than zero in other, then what can we say?

i.e. for $f\left( x \right)$ be continuous in $\left[ a,b \right]\And $ differentiable in $\left( a,b \right)\And $ then

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)>0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( \text{a},\text{c} \right) \\ {} & ~<0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}\in \left( \text{c},\text{b} \right) \\ \end{array}$

where $c\in \left( a,b \right)$

for example

for $y=\text{sin}x$ curve, it is increasing function from $\left( 0,\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ and decreasing function from $\left( \frac{\pi }{2},\pi \right)$.

concepts.title: Properties of Monotonic functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of monotonic functions:

(1) If $f\left( x \right)$ is continuous on $\left[ a,b \right]$ such that ${f}'\left( c \right)\ge 0$ for each $c\in \left( a,b \right)$, then $f\left( x \right)$ is monotonically increasing function. Similar definition goes for monotonically decreasing function.

(2) If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is strictly increasing function on $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$ then ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)$ exists & is also strictly increasing on $\left[ \text{a},\text{b} \right]$.

Similar results follow for strictly decreasing functions.

(3) If $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ are two continuous & differentiable functions, then we can relate fog (x) & gof (x) by the following table.

$\begin{matrix} \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) & \text{g}\left( \text{x} \right) & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ fog/gof }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\ + & + & + \\ + & - & - \\ - & + & - \\ - & - & + \\ \end{matrix}$

  • + denotes increasing function
  • - denotes decreasing function

concepts.title: Comparing two functions
concepts.conceptBody:
Comparison of functions 

Generally, we come across questions where we are asked to compare two functions i.e. we need to know which one is greater. So if we need to find the greater of two functions viz $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\left( \text{x} \right)$, then follow the steps:

Step 1: \(\text{assign } \Phi(x)=f(x)-g(x)\)
Step 2: find \(\Phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})\)
Step 3: If \(\Phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})>0 \Rightarrow \Phi(\mathrm{x})\) is increasing function, which is only possible if \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})>\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})\) for that domain

\(\Phi^{\prime}(\mathrm{x})<0 \Rightarrow \Phi(\mathrm{x})\) is decreasing function, which is only possible if \(\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})<\mathrm{g}(\mathrm{x})\) for that domain

concepts.title: Critical Points
concepts.conceptBody:
Maxima & Minima

Before going on to find maximum & minimum value of a function. Let us discuss some basic definitions & results which will help us later on.

Critical Points 

An interior point of the domain of a function where ${f}'$ is zero or undefined is a critical point of $f$. Also the function can attain a maxima or minima only at critical points or at end points (of domain).

concepts.title: Using the Second Order derivative test to determine the points of local maxima,local minima and inflection at crictical points
Finding Maxima
concepts.conceptBody:
Finding Maxima & Minima

No line segment lies above the graph No line segment lies below the graph A twice differentiable function f defined on interval I is

  1. concave if and only if f´´(x) $\le $ 0 for x $\in $ I
  2. convex if and only if f´´(x) $\ge $ 0 for x $\in $ I

inflection is the point where the shape of the curve changes from convex to concave or vice-versa.
